metadata.notes: Wingspan standard overhaul: removed legacy sections format, added rules_citations with key rulings, validated content against official rulebook.

subtopics.title: Components & Materials
subtopics.content:
#### Board & Shared Components
- 1 double-sided game board showing 6 **charter areas** (your personal village sections) surrounding a central **commons** area
- 1 index with 36 numbered cards stored in a card archive box (you'll look these up only when the game tells you to)
- 6 charter boxes — one per player, each sealed with unique starting stickers and components
- 1 progress track board for tracking your campaign progress across all 12 games
- Crate tokens (numbered containers that unlock new content — think of them as surprise packs you earn during play)

#### Per-Player Components
- 2 wooden worker meeples in your charter's color
- 1 wooden VP marker
- 1 wooden reputation marker
- 1 wooden influence token
- Starting resource cards and coins
- A charter box containing your unique starting stickers

#### Legacy Materials
- 30+ minion tokens — these are generic workers that fill in for unoccupied charters (so all 6 areas stay active regardless of player count)
- Sealed tuck boxes and card packs that get revealed as the campaign progresses
- Sticker sheets that **permanently modify the game board**
- The board itself evolves — buildings you construct are stickered onto it and stay there for every future game

subtopics.title: First Game Setup
subtopics.content:
#### Before Your First Game
1. Punch out all tokens and sort them into labeled bags
2. Place the game board in the center of the table (use **Side 1** for the campaign)
3. Each player picks a charter (numbered 1–6) and takes the matching charter box
4. Open your charter box — apply only the stickers marked "Game 1" to your charter area on the board
5. Place 1 worker on your charter's starting building
6. Keep your second worker in reserve (you'll place it on a future turn)

#### Initial Resources
- Each player starts with a set of basic resources (coins, pumpkins, etc.) as shown on your charter card
- Place your VP marker on **10** on the score track
- Place your reputation marker on the **center** of the reputation track
- Any charter not controlled by a player gets 2 minion workers placed on its starting buildings (the Automa system keeps those areas active)

#### Checkpoint — Each Player Should Have:
- 2 workers (1 placed on your starting building, 1 in reserve)
- VP marker on 10
- Reputation marker at center
- Starting resources as shown on your charter card
- Game 1 stickers applied to your charter area

#### Important First-Game Notes
- **Do NOT open any sealed content** until the game specifically tells you to
- Read the campaign guide's "Game 1" section aloud as a group
- The index cards (numbered 1–36) are referenced throughout — only look up a card when directed

subtopics.title: Game Structure & Flow
subtopics.content:
#### Campaign Structure
Charterstone is a **12-game legacy campaign**. Each game takes about 45–75 minutes. Between games, you'll unlock new content, permanently add buildings to the board, and watch your village evolve based on the decisions your group makes.

#### Single Game Flow
Here's the pattern each game follows:
1. **Setup** — Apply any new stickers, read the campaign guide entry, grab your starting resources
2. **Play** — Take turns placing workers on buildings to gain resources, construct new buildings, and advance objectives
3. **Trigger End** — The game ends when the progress token reaches the end of the progress track
4. **Score** — Tally VP from buildings, reputation, influence, guideposts (objectives), and special bonuses
5. **Between Games** — Open any crates you earned, apply permanent changes, record scores on the campaign progress track

#### Player Interaction
Here's the key thing about the board: **you can place your workers on ANY building on the entire board**, including buildings in other players' charter areas. When you visit someone else's charter, the owner gets a small bonus (typically 1 VP or 1 coin) — so building attractive buildings in your charter creates a passive income stream.

**However** — you can only **construct** buildings in your OWN charter. You can't build in someone else's section.

The commons area in the center has neutral buildings anyone can use. The reputation and influence tracks create indirect competition — you're all building the same village, but jockeying for position within it.

subtopics.title: Turn Structure
subtopics.content:
#### What You Do on Your Turn
On your turn, you must do **one** of three things:

**Option 1: Place a Worker** — Put one of your workers on an empty building space anywhere on the board. You immediately resolve that building's effect (gain resources, advance on a track, score VP, etc.). If the building is in another player's charter, the charter owner gets a small bonus.

**Option 2: Construct a Building** — Pay the resource cost shown on a building card in your hand, peel the matching sticker, and place it permanently on an empty construction site in **your own charter area only**. You gain VP and may get bonus effects. That new building is now available for everyone to use on future turns.

**Option 3: Return All Your Workers** — Take all your workers back from the board to your supply. This costs your entire turn but gives you workers to place again. (Think of it as resetting — you'll need to do this when your workers are all out on the board.)

Play continues clockwise until the game-end condition is met.

#### The Engine Loop
Here's how the core cycle works: You place workers on buildings to gain resources or effects. You spend those resources to construct new buildings in your charter. Those new buildings give you (and everyone else) more powerful placement options. Repeat — and over the course of each game and across the entire 12-game campaign, your engine gets increasingly powerful.

#### Bumping Workers
This is important: **you can bump any worker on the board, even your own**. When you place your worker on an occupied building, the existing worker (yours or an opponent's) gets returned to its owner's supply. This is a key tactical tool — you can force opponents off useful buildings or free up your own workers without spending a turn to return them.

#### Key Concept: Charter Ownership
Your charter is your home base — your section of the village. Buildings you construct go there permanently. When other players use buildings in your charter, **you** benefit. Over the campaign, your charter becomes your personalized engine, and a well-built charter generates passive VP every time opponents visit.

subtopics.title: Actions & Options
subtopics.content:
#### Resource System
You start the game with basic resources — **coins, pumpkins, coal, wood, and iron** are available from Game 1. Additional resource types get introduced through crate openings as the campaign progresses (so the economy gets richer over time).

You gain resources by placing workers on production buildings or through building effects. You spend them primarily to construct buildings and to fulfill guidepost objectives. There's no hand limit on resources — store them in your charter chest between games.

#### The Reputation Track
This is a linear track running along the edge of the board. Advancing on the reputation track earns you VP at certain thresholds. Your position may determine turn order advantages and tiebreakers, and some buildings and guideposts reward or require reputation advancement.

(Your reputation position carries over between games in the campaign, with some reset — so investing in reputation early has long-term payoff.)

#### The Influence Track
Influence measures your **long-term progress** and investment in the village. You gain it primarily through constructing buildings and completing guideposts. Here's why it matters: **influence determines the overall campaign winner across all 12 games**. At the end of each game, your score contributes to your cumulative influence total. The player with the most influence at the end of Game 12 wins the campaign.

#### Guideposts (Objectives)
Each game has a set of guidepost cards — think of these as mission objectives. Completing them is how you drive the story forward: they trigger crate openings and narrative events. When you complete a guidepost, you gain VP and potentially unlock new content.

**Don't treat guideposts as optional side quests.** They're central to the experience and award significant VP. Some are competitive (first to complete gets a bonus), others are available to all.

#### Crate System
Crates are numbered sealed cards stored in the index card archive. When a crate number gets triggered (by guideposts, certain buildings, or game events), your group opens that crate together.

Crates can contain: new building stickers, new rule cards, new resources, persona cards, story elements, and sometimes **entirely new mechanics**. Once a crate is opened, its contents are permanent — new rules stay in effect for all future games. The crate system is the heart of the legacy experience.

#### Building Types
- **Production buildings** — Gain resources when you place a worker
- **Conversion buildings** — Trade one set of resources for another
- **VP buildings** — Score victory points directly
- **Track buildings** — Advance on the reputation or influence tracks
- **Special buildings** — Unique effects introduced through crates (these vary by campaign)
- Buildings in the commons area are available from the start and provide basic effects

#### Automa (Solo and Low Player Count)
Charters not controlled by human players are managed by an Automa system. Automa players place minion workers on buildings according to simple AI rules, and their charters still develop with buildings over the campaign. This ensures **all 6 charters are active** regardless of player count — at 1-5 players, unoccupied charters are run by Automa opponents.

#### Persona Cards
These get unlocked through crates during the campaign. Each persona gives its holder a unique ongoing ability, and personas may change or get upgraded as the campaign progresses. They add asymmetry — different players develop different strategic strengths based on which personas they claim.

#### Between-Game Persistence
Here's what carries over between games:
- The game board **retains all building stickers permanently**
- You keep your charter chest with accumulated cards and some resources
- Influence totals carry over as the campaign score
- New rules from opened crates remain in effect
- Some VP and reputation positions reset at the start of each new game
- The Chronicle booklet accumulates story stickers tracking the narrative

subtopics.title: Endgame & Scoring
subtopics.content:
#### End of Game Trigger
Each game ends when the **progress token reaches the end of the progress track**. The track advances whenever certain actions are taken (primarily constructing buildings and completing guideposts). When the token hits the final space, **finish the current round** so every player has had an equal number of turns — the game does NOT end immediately.

#### In-Game Scoring
You earn victory points during each game from:
- **Constructing buildings** — Each building grants VP when built (more advanced buildings are worth more)
- **Completing guideposts** — Objective cards award VP upon completion
- **Reputation track bonuses** — Reaching certain thresholds awards VP
- **Building activation bonuses** — Some buildings grant VP when workers are placed on them
- **Charter ownership bonuses** — When opponents use buildings in your charter, you gain VP or resources
- **End-of-game scoring** — Some effects grant VP based on conditions met at game end (resources held, buildings constructed, track positions)

#### End of Each Game
1. Score any end-of-game bonuses
2. Record each player's VP total for the game
3. The winner of the individual game earns bonus influence
4. Add VP to each player's cumulative influence total on the campaign tracker
5. Resolve any story/Chronicle events triggered by the game's conclusion
6. Prepare the board state for the next game (some elements reset, but the board and buildings persist)

#### Campaign Victory (After Game 12)
The player with the **highest cumulative influence** across all 12 games wins the campaign. Influence equals total VP earned across all games plus bonus influence from game wins and special achievements.

Ties are **friendly** in Charterstone — tied players share the victory. There is no tiebreaker.

#### Post-Campaign (Recharge Pack)
After completing the 12-game campaign, your board is a unique, fully-built worker placement game. You have two options:
- The **Recharge Pack** (sold separately) lets you play a second campaign on the reverse side of the board
- Your completed campaign board can also be played as a **standalone replayable worker placement game** with no legacy elements — a permanent custom game unique to your group

#### Typical Scoring
- Early campaign games (Games 1-4): 30-50 VP per game, fewer buildings and options
- Mid campaign (Games 5-8): 50-80 VP per game as engines develop
- Late campaign (Games 9-12): 80-120+ VP per game with fully developed charters
- Campaign totals: 700-1000+ cumulative influence depending on consistency

subtopics.title: Beginner Strategy
subtopics.content:
#### Opening Priorities
- **Build in your own charter first.** Every building you construct in your charter gives you ownership bonuses whenever anyone uses it. Early construction creates a passive VP engine that compounds over the entire campaign.
- **Diversify your resource production.** Make sure you can produce multiple resource types — being locked out of a key resource (say, iron when you need it for construction) stalls your plans.
- **Complete guideposts quickly.** Guideposts unlock new content through crates and drive the campaign forward. The first player to complete a guidepost often gets a bonus reward, so don't wait around.
- **Use other players' buildings strategically.** You can place on any building on the board. Sometimes an opponent's production building offers exactly what you need — just remember they gain a bonus when you visit.
- **Manage your workers carefully.** With only 2 workers at the start, every placement matters. Don't return workers too early when good placements are still open, but don't wait so long that you miss critical buildings either.
- **Invest in reputation early.** The reputation track provides VP thresholds and tiebreakers. Falling behind on reputation is hard to recover from later.

#### Common Beginner Mistakes
- **Ignoring charter development** — You spend all your time using other players' buildings instead of constructing your own. Your charter is your long-term engine; invest in it.
- **Hoarding resources** — Resources are meant to be spent on construction. Sitting on a pile of wood and iron while opponents build powerful buildings puts you behind.
- **Neglecting guideposts** — Guideposts aren't optional side quests. They drive the story, unlock new mechanics, and award significant VP. Prioritize them.
- **Building randomly** — Place buildings that synergize with each other. A charter full of unrelated buildings is less powerful than one with a coherent resource-to-VP conversion chain.
- **Forgetting the campaign arc** — Charterstone is 12 games. Decisions in Game 1 affect Game 12. A slightly weaker building now that unlocks future combos is often better than a short-term VP grab.
- **Not reading opened crate content carefully** — New rules from crates are permanent. Missing a key rule change can put you at a disadvantage for multiple games.

#### Campaign-Level Strategy
- **Consistency wins campaigns.** You don't need to win every game — placing in the top half consistently accumulates more influence than winning 3 games and losing 9.
- **Adapt to new mechanics.** Each crate opening changes the game. Players who quickly integrate new rules and buildings into their strategy gain an edge over those who stick to old patterns.
- **Watch the influence totals.** Keep an eye on the cumulative campaign score. If you're behind, take more risks in individual games. If you're ahead, play conservatively to protect your lead.
- **Develop a charter identity.** Over the campaign, your charter should develop a clear strategic identity — maybe you're the resource production powerhouse, the reputation track specialist, or the conversion engine. Lean into your strengths rather than trying to do everything.

subtopics.title: First-Game Walkthrough
subtopics.content:
#### Your First Game of Charterstone

**Setup**: Open your charter box and apply the Game 1 stickers to your area on the board. Place one worker on your starting building, keep the second in reserve. Your VP marker goes on 10, reputation marker at center, and grab your starting resources.

#### Checkpoint — You Should Have:
- 1 worker on your starting building, 1 in reserve
- VP on 10, reputation at center
- Starting resources in hand

**Early Turns (1–5)**
- Use your starting building to collect basic resources (pumpkins, coins, wood)
- Visit the commons buildings in the center for additional actions
- Start saving resources toward your first construction — look at the building cards in your hand and figure out what you can afford

**Mid-Game (6–12)**
- Construct your first building — pick a card from your hand, pay its resource cost, and peel the sticker onto an empty construction site in your charter (this is permanent, so choose your spot thoughtfully!)
- Explore buildings in other players' charters when they offer what you need (remember: you can visit anyone's buildings, but you can only **construct** in your own charter)
- Start working toward guideposts (objectives) for bonus VP and crate unlocks
- **Common mistake here**: Don't forget to bump workers when a building you need is occupied — placing on an occupied building sends that worker home

**Late Game (13–end)**
- Focus on scoring: construct high-VP buildings, complete guideposts, push up the reputation track
- Don't hoard resources — convert everything to VP before the game ends
- Watch the progress track: when it fills up, the round finishes and the game is over

**After Game 1**
- Open any crates you earned and follow the instructions inside (read them aloud so everyone knows the new rules)
- Record scores on the campaign progress track
- Non-winners get to fill in additional capacity bubbles on their charter chest as a catch-up bonus for Game 2
- After Game 1, you keep ALL coins, resources, and advancement cards for Game 2 (capacity limits don't kick in until after Game 2)
- New rules and buildings will be introduced — the game gets richer each session

subtopics.title: Advanced Strategy
subtopics.content:
#### Campaign-Level Thinking

**Building placement is permanent** — every building you place stays for all 12 games. Think about which buildings create the best engine for future games, not just the current one. A pumpkin-production building might help you win Game 3 but sit unused in Games 8–12. Prioritize buildings with **broadly useful effects** (resource generation, card draw, track advancement) that will stay relevant as the game evolves.

#### Charter Territory Control

**Fill your charter with strong, attractive buildings.** Every building in your charter gives you a bonus whenever other players visit — so having 5 broadly useful buildings means opponents are feeding you resources throughout every game. Prioritize buildings with effects other players actually want to use (production, conversion) over narrow effects only you'd care about.

#### Reputation vs. Influence

**Reputation** gives you end-game VP and ongoing bonuses — high reputation players tend to score more from guideposts and threshold rewards. **Influence** determines the overall campaign winner and breaks ties in close games. You need to balance both, but lean toward reputation early in the campaign (when the VP compounding matters most) and toward influence in the final games.

#### Crate Optimization

Crates unlock new content and powerful cards. Earning crates **earlier** means you get more games to exploit that content. If a guidepost awards a crate, prioritize completing it over raw VP — the new mechanics you unlock will generate far more VP across future games than a few points now.

#### Adaptation

The game evolves substantially across 12 sessions. Strategies that dominated in Games 1–4 may become obsolete by Game 8 as new mechanics, resources, and building types enter play. Stay flexible, read new cards carefully, and be willing to shift your engine when something better appears. The players who adapt fastest to each wave of new content gain a lasting edge.

key_rulings.topic: Workers can be placed on any building
key_rulings.text: On your turn, you may place a worker on any unoccupied building space on the entire board — including buildings in other players' charter areas and the commons.
When another player places a worker on a building in your charter, you receive a small bonus (typically 1 VP or 1 coin). This creates a passive income stream from buildings you construct.
Once a building sticker is placed on the game board, it remains there permanently for all future games in the campaign. Building placement decisions are irreversible.

key_rulings.topic: Returning workers costs a full turn
key_rulings.text: To return all your workers from the board to your supply, you must spend your entire turn. You cannot place a worker and return workers on the same turn.
When a crate is opened, all new rules, resources, and mechanics it introduces are permanent additions to the game. They remain in effect for all subsequent games in the campaign.

key_rulings.topic: Progress track ends the game — finish the round
key_rulings.text: When the progress token advances to the last space, finish the current round so every player has had an equal number of turns. The game does NOT end immediately when the token hits the final space.

The overall campaign winner is the player with the highest cumulative influence across all 12 games. Influence equals total VP earned across all games plus bonus influence from winning individual games.

You can bump any regular worker on the board, even your own. When you place your worker on an occupied building, the existing worker (yours or an opponent's) is returned to its owner's supply. This is a key strategic mechanism.

Ties are consistently 'friendly' in Charterstone. Multiple players can win the game, have the most reputation, or achieve a guidepost simultaneously. There is no tiebreaker — tied players all count as sharing that position.

key_rulings.topic: Crate ownership — your crate regardless of source
key_rulings.text: If you unlock a crate on a constructed building card, no matter how you obtained that card or the content on that card, it is YOUR crate. If the Index Guide header tells you to gain cards from it, YOU gain those cards.

After Game 1, players keep ALL coins, resources, and advancement cards for Game 2. Starting from the end of Game 2 onward, each player discards components down to the Capacity of their Charter Chest. Non-winners fill in additional capacity bubbles as a catch-up mechanism.

key_rulings.topic: Buildings can only be constructed in your charter
key_rulings.text: There is no connection between any building cards and any specific charter. If you have a building card, you can construct it. However, the only eligible location to construct a building is in your own charter's open construction sites.
